mainEntity.name: Do I need to shoot in RAW to follow this workflow?

acceptedAnswer.text: Strictly no — the same seven stages apply to JPEGs. Practically yes — RAW gives far more headroom to recover detail, adjust white balance, and shape tone without artefacts. JPEGs have already been processed by your camera and discarded data. If you are serious about your photographs, shoot RAW.

mainEntity.name: How long should editing one photo take?

acceptedAnswer.text: Anywhere from two minutes for a clean file that needs only basic-panel work, to an hour or more for complex landscapes with bracketed exposures, masking, and Photoshop work. The benefit of a workflow is consistency, not speed. If you find yourself spending more than 30 minutes still tinkering, it is usually a Stage 3 intent problem rather than a tool problem.

mainEntity.name: Can I use this workflow in Photoshop, Capture One, or Affinity Photo instead of Lightroom?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. The seven stages are software-agnostic. Every serious RAW editor has equivalents to import, organisation, develop, local adjustments and export. Capture One, Affinity Photo and DxO PhotoLab all support the same framework. Adobe is the most common, which is why we teach it, but the principles transfer.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between global and local adjustments?

acceptedAnswer.text: Global adjustments affect the entire image — exposure, white balance, contrast, basic panel sliders. Local adjustments affect specific areas via brushes, gradients, or masks. Globals come first because they set the tonal direction of the whole frame; once globals feel right, locals support the intent set in Stage 3.

mainEntity.name: How do I know when an edit is finished?

acceptedAnswer.text: Compare the result to the brief you wrote in Stage 3 — does the image communicate the right thing, emphasise what caught your eye, and convey the feeling you wanted? If yes, you are done. If you cannot tell, walk away and return with fresh eyes. The before/after toggle is also useful: a healthy edit shows clear improvement without announcing itself as heavily processed.

For starting points yes; for finished edits no. A preset usefully applies typical lens correction, white balance, and starting tonal direction in one click. But every image is different, and a preset cannot answer the three intent questions for a specific frame. Use presets to skip the chores; do the creative work yourself.

mainEntity.name: What is the right export setting for printing versus sharing online?

acceptedAnswer.text: Online: sRGB colour space, 1024–2048 pixels on the long edge, JPEG quality 80–90, output sharpening for screen. Print: lab-specified colour space (most labs sRGB, pro labs may accept Adobe RGB), native PPI for the print size, output sharpening for matte or glossy paper. Print sharpening is more aggressive than screen sharpening because the print process softens detail.

Three habits help. First, Stage 3 — a clear brief gives you a stop signal. Second, the before/after toggle — compare your edit to the original RAW regularly; if the gap feels uncomfortable, you have drifted. Third, walk-away time — your eye adapts to the screen. Do not compare your edits to heavily-processed Instagram posts; compare them to your own intent.
